results.title: Instagram
results.content:
Alibaba just did something nobody in India is talking about.  
  
It quietly became one of the world’s leading AI video companies.  
  
Most people think the AI race is still GPT vs Claude.  
  
I think the battlefield has already changed.  
  
It’s no longer model versus model.  
  
It’s workflow versus workflow.  
  
While everyone debates chatbots, Alibaba has been building AI video infrastructure.  
  
And it’s working.  
  
Its Wan model has climbed into the top tier of global AI video rankings, ahead of several models that dominated headlines just months ago.  
  
That isn’t just a model update.  
  
It’s a signal.  
  
The next AI winners won’t necessarily be the companies with the smartest models.  
  
They’ll be the companies that own how AI gets used every day. [...] They create entirely new industries.  
  
[Alibaba, Wan AI, AI video generation, artificial intelligence, generative AI, content creation, AI infrastructure, creator economy, video AI, AI workflows, synthetic media, AI tools, technology trends, future of AI, digital content] [...] Video creation.  
  
Advertising.  
  
Content production.  
  
Creative workflows.  
  
That’s where value compounds.  
  
History shows the best technology doesn’t always win.  
  
The company with the best distribution usually does.  
  
And right now, China isn’t just building AI models.  
  
It’s building ecosystems around them.  
  
Most founders are still thinking about chatbots.  
  
The next opportunity may be building on top of AI-generated video.  
  
Because the biggest platform shifts don’t create one winner.  
  
They create entirely new industries.

results.title: What Happened When AI Rewrote Meta’s Infrastructure Playbook - VAST Data
results.content:
Thought Leadership

Sep 11, 2025

# What Happened When AI Rewrote Meta’s Infrastructure Playbook

Authored by

Nicole Hemsoth Prickett

> Meta is emptying datacenters, wiring gigawatt clusters, and battling hardware sprawl as AI training jobs push GPUs, networks, and power to their limits.

Meta’s Vice President of Infrastructure, Yee Jiun Song, has spent the two decades pushing the limits of datacenter design, and told the packed room this week at the AI Infra Summit that the playbook for web and mobile scale has broken under AI.

He explained that recommendation engines and LLMs now require clusters so large they consume entire facilities, with jobs so brittle a single GPU can topple them, and with hardware ecosystems so fragmented that only open standards can hold the pieces together. [...] But AI introduced a new class of really big problems.

For years, he says, Facebook products relied on the bounded nature of the social graph (show users what their friends liked or what their communities discussed) but the rise of short-form video changed everything. Relevance could no longer be derived from human networks. Every uploaded video had to be evaluated against every user’s interests, a computationally unbounded problem that CPUs couldn’t handle. [...] By 2022, competitive training jobs demanded synchronous operation across thousands of GPUs. What had once been 128-device clusters expanded to 2,000 and then 14,000 devices. “For the first time, we were regularly dealing with training jobs where we needed thousands of GPUs to run synchronously… any single stranding GPU across the whole job failed,” Song said.

Engineers who once optimized for server reboots now had to make sure multi-week training runs could survive the weakest link in tens of thousands of devices.

Meta response? Building 24,000-GPU clusters, designed to fill every watt of power and every square foot of cooling capacity in single datacenter buildings. And even that wasn’t enough.

results.title:

But the infrastructure is stuck in the mud.

I see a massive gap in the market right now. 
Founders are waiting for tools that make… | Srini Annamaraju | 62 comments
results.content:
Most people think AI development is moving at light speed.
But the infrastructure is stuck in the mud.
I see a massive gap in the market right now.
Founders are waiting for tools that make training easy.
We are still doing things the hard way: → Manually visualizing terabytes of data → Fighting with busted instances → Patching together broken open source tools
The opportunity is not in the model. It is in the shovel.
Industry is looking for products that solve the real problems:
↳ APIs that abstract training databases
↳ Environments built specifically for ML workflows
↳ Tools that handle the mess so we can focus on the product
As models specialize, this layer becomes the most important part of the stack. This is how software will be built in the future. [...] If you are working on this, you are sitting on a goldmine. Do not let this opportunity pass.
✍️ Your insights can make a difference
♻️ Share this post if it speaks to you, and follow me for more. [...] Agree & Join LinkedIn

results.title: Train and deploy generative AI faster with MosaicML and Oracle | cloud-infrastructure
results.content:
Building this complex infrastructure cost-effectively is extremely difficult. It requires experts who possess a deep understanding of various machine learning (ML) frameworks and tools and know how to efficiently utilize these accelerators. You must provision and maintain the computing resources, requiring complex tools to enable orchestration and coordination across multiple users. At scale, hardware failures are common, placing an even greater burden on IT departments to manage bespoke resources and diagnose complex issues. [...] Training generative AI, such as LLMs, is expensive and complicated. For this reason, most generative AI companies devote most of their resources and expertise to training a single model, then sell commoditized services around the capabilities of that model. Some of these companies even let you domain-tune their models to improve quality. They deal with the complexities of training and host the model behind an API while you access the benefits and get billed by use. Problem solved, right?

## Business data and third-party APIs [...] OCI includes numerous capabilities for AI, including AI infrastructure. OCI Compute virtual machines (VMs) and bare metal NVIDIA GPU instances can power applications for computer vision, natural language processing (NLP), recommendation systems, and more. For training LLMs at-scale, OCI Supercluster provides ultra-low-latency cluster networking, high-performance computing (HPC) storage, and OCI Compute bare metal instances powered by NVIDIA A100 Tensor Core GPUs and ConnectX SmartNICs.

Figure 4: OCI Supercluster

## MosaicML and OCI, better together

results.title: UWS Train Has By Far the Most Broken Air Conditioning Complaints of Any in NYC: Data
results.content:
Additionally, subway car mechanics told Gothamist that the design of the air conditioning units on the 1 line is “antiquated and prone to breakdowns,” and that mechanics work on 1 trains at the MTA’s 240th Street train yard, which has failing infrastructure and slows the speed of possible work.

“With 1.2 billion trips over hundreds of millions of miles last year, fewer than one in every half-million riders reported a warm subway car, and some of those came contemporaneously about the same car,” Bill Amarosa Jr., NYC Transit’s senior vice president of subways, told Gothamist in a statement. “There’s a reason complaints dropped 21% last year from the year before — subway air conditioners are regularly checked and when they fail, usually on a decades-old car, they get fixed.” [...] Along with the 1 train, five of the 10 train lines with the most heat-related complaints service the Upper West Side in some fashion, including the 3 train, which had the third most complaints of any in New York City.

In terms of a possible explanation for the 1 train’s sweaty conditions, the line still deploys R62 train cars, which are among the oldest in the MTA’s system and were built when subway air conditioning was still a new development. These cars are expected to be phased out in the coming years. [...] So please, take a moment to report any train issues via the MTA app and hopefully we can all experience fewer hot subway trips.

Jim in NYC

Surely the train in the photo is pulling out of the station, rather than into it, no? (That’s OK—I’ll show myself out.)

Sandro

This is true. I’d say 25-35% of my 1 train rides are unairconditioned or underairconditioned. It’s gotten commonplace enough that one can’t use the typical locals’ tell of avoiding the empty cars, as people largely suck it up and go even in warm cars.

fran

The 1 train has had the same car with broken AC for years. It’s super annoying. I can’t understand how/why it isn’t fixed by now.

Chris

This is the link to the complaints form, which includes no air conditioning.
